coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal determination of dopamine by the use of Cu-Mn-O microcrystals and C-dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jian
Du, Ran
Liu, Wei
Yao, Lang
Ding, Fang
Zou, Ping
Wang, Yanying
Wang, Xianxiang
Zhao, Qingbiao
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal oxides are potent catalytic materials owing to the high electron transport efficiency and high catalytic activity properties, thus they are widely used as mimic enzyme material. In this work, Cu-Mn-O catalytic material was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis of Cu doped dumbbell MnCO3 microcrystals as precursor and calcination of the precursor. The morphology and structure were characterized by TEM, SEM, XRD and XPS. With the oxidase activity, the Cu-Mn-O material can catalyze the oxidation of colorless substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to generate the blue product of oxTMB. The absorption of oxTMB overlaps with the emission of the C-dots due to the fluorescence inner filter effect, leading to the reduction of the fluorescence emission intensity from the carbon quantum dots. Moreover, dopamine can inhibit the activity of Cu-Mn-O catalytic material, leading to the fading of the blue color of the oxTMB, and the fluorescence from carbon quantum dots is restored, thus achieving the goal of colorimetric and fluorescence dual-signal determination for dopamine. The linear range of the colorimetric method is 0–75 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5 μM, while the linear range for the fluorescence method is 0–100 μM with the LOD of 0.125 μM. The Cu-Mn-O material has high potential in biomedical analysis of dopamine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric
Fluorometric
Dopamine
Mimetric oxidase
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Activity and kinetics study of the oxidase of Cu-MnxOy
Synthesis and characterization of Cu-MnxOy and C-dots
Study on oxidase activity, mechanism and kinetics of Cu-MnxOy
The selectivity of the system to dopamine
Determination of dopamine in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B, C) SEM images of Cu-MnxOy and (D) TEM images of Cu-MnxOy (E) Elemental mapping of Cu-MnxOy (F) Elemental mapping of Cu (G) Elemental mapping of Mn (H) Elemental mapping of O.
(A) HRTEM image of C-dots (B) The size distribution of C-dots.
(A) XPS spectrum of Cu-MnxOy (B) XPS spectrum of Cu2p (C) XPS spectrum of Mn2p (D) XPS spectrum of O1s.
(A) The effects of carbon dots and Cu-MnxOy on TMB at room temperature for 15 min (B) UV–Vis absorbance of the sensing system with the addition of different radical scavengers.

(A) UV/Vis absorption spectra of Cu–MnxOy/C-dots (black line), Cu–MnxOy/C-dots/TMB (red line), Cu–MnxOy/C-dots/TMB/DA (blue line) (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of Cu–MnxOy/TMB/C-dots (black line), Cu–MnxOy/C-dots (red line), Cu–MnxOy/TMB/DA/C-dots (blue line), Cu–MnxOy/DA/C-dots (pink line), C-dots (green line), DA/C-dots (dark blue line), TMB/C-dots (bluish violet line), Cu–MnxOy/TMB/DA (purple line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) The visual color changes and dose-response of DA under the optimal conditions. inset: photographs of the system with DA concentration from 0 to 100 μM (B) The plots of ΔA versus DA concentrations (C) The linear relation between ΔA and DA concentrations in the range of 0–75 μM (Inset: The linear relation between ΔA and DA concentrations in the range 0–5 μM) (ΔA = A0–A, A0 and A mean the absorbance intensity at 652 nm without and with DA, respectively).
(A) The emission spectra of DA/TMB/C-dots/Cu-MnxOy with various concentration of DA (B) The calibration curves for DA determination.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route for Cu-MnxOy and C-dots.
Comparison to existing methods for analysis of DA.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal determination of dopamine by the use of Cu-Mn-O microcrystals and C-dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an, 625014, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology
Guangdong Research Center for Interfacial Engineering of Functional Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Department of Optoelectronics, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices
Ministry of Education, Department of Optoelectronics

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work and are joint first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mechanism for the determination of dopamine based on Cu-MnxOy and C-dots with TMB.

Transition metal oxides are potent catalytic materials owing to the high electron transport efficiency and high catalytic activity properties, thus they are widely used as mimic enzyme material. In this work, Cu-Mn-O catalytic material was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis of Cu doped dumbbell MnCO3 microcrystals as precursor and calcination of the precursor. The morphology and structure were characterized by TEM, SEM, XRD and XPS. With the oxidase activity, the Cu-Mn-O material can catalyze the oxidation of colorless substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to generate the blue product of oxTMB. The absorption of oxTMB overlaps with the emission of the C-dots due to the fluorescence inner filter effect, leading to the reduction of the fluorescence emission intensity from the carbon quantum dots. Moreover, dopamine can inhibit the activity of Cu-Mn-O catalytic material, leading to the fading of the blue color of the oxTMB, and the fluorescence from carbon quantum dots is restored, thus achieving the goal of colorimetric and fluorescence dual-signal determination for dopamine. The linear range of the colorimetric method is 0–75 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5 μM, while the linear range for the fluorescence method is 0–100 μM with the LOD of 0.125 μM. The Cu-Mn-O material has high potential in biomedical analysis of dopamine.

ce:section.ce:para: As one essential hormone in human body, dopamine can take part in a variety of physiological processes and is necessary to maintain the normal activity of the organism. Abnormal level of dopamine will cause hormonal imbalance and certain diseases. Previous reports have demonstrated that a rise in dopamine levels in the striatum will result in the onset of schizophrenia, while a decrease in dopamine levels can lead to increased levels of iron in the brain, which is related with neurological degeneration and Parkinson's disease [1,2]. Therefore, determination of dopamine levels is of great significance. Several approaches were proposed for the analysis of dopamine, including colorimetric [3], electrochemistry [4], fluorescence [5], chemiluminescence [6] and chromatography [7].

Colorimetric method is simple and efficient, in which the specific enzymes can catalyze the transformation of the substrate, resulting in apparent color change. However, natural enzymes are easily affected by environmental factors, easy to inactivate, require relatively strict detection conditions and of relatively high cost. Therefore, it is necessary to develop proper substitutes for natural enzymes. It was reported that Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibit peroxidase activity and can catalyze the reaction [8].Synthesis of Fe3O4 nanoparticles is simple and low cost, and a series of inorganic nano-enzymes have been developed. The colorless TMB can be oxidized to produce oxTMB with blue color by nanomaterials with oxidase-like activities with the aid of reactive oxygen species. The catalysts include transition metal oxides [9], metal complexes [10], metal particles [11], perovskite-type catalytic material [3], and metal organic framework [12]. Studies have shown that good morphology and high specific surface area are important for catalytic activity. Therefore, well-designed topography and highly specific surfaces of catalytic materials are desired.

Manganese oxide has attracted wide attention because of the low toxicity and low cost. Doping of manganese oxide by other metal ions can improve the catalytic efficiency [13,14]. Therefore, the synthesis of manganese oxide doped with copper ions by micro dumbbell manganese carbonate is considered. The synthesis of C-dots is simple and low-cost. C-dots have high biocompatibility, non-toxicity, viable cell permeability and strong fluorescence emission [15]. They were widely used in analysis of imaging and analytical detection [16,17].

In this study, the Cu-Mn-O catalytic material was synthesized without the need of stabilizer in the synthetic process. The Cu-Mn-O material can catalyze the oxidation of the colorless substrate TMB forming a blue oxidized product, due to its oxidase activity. Based on the fluorescence inner filter effect, the absorption of oxTMB has overlap with the emission peaks of carbon quantum dots, thus it can lead to the fluorescence emission intensity reduction from the carbon quantum dots. Moreover, dopamine can inhibit the activity of Cu-Mn-O catalytic materials, causing the blue TMB oxidation products to fade, so that the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots is restored. Therefore, a fluorescent colorimetric dual-signal system is established for the detection of dopamine. This convenient and sensitive method with broad linear range has high potential for biomedical applications.

Copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O, purity ≥99%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, purity ≥99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, purity ≥99%), dopamine, uric acid, urea, tryptophan, glutamate, glycine, arginine, histidine, lactic acid, fructose, sucrose, glucose, galactose, phenylalanine, glutamic acid, phenol, dichlorophenol, glycerol, dopa, resorcinol, hydroquinone, 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid and acarbose hydrate were obtained from Chron Chemical (ChengDu, China, http://www.chronchem.com/en/). Tannic acid (C76H52O46) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China, http://tjguangfu.company.lookchem.cn/). 3,3′,5,5′tetramethylbenzidine(TMB), fetal bovine serum, polyethylene imide (Mw = 600, purity ≥ 99%), t-Butanol (TBA), superoxide dismutase (SOD), p-benzoquinone (PBQ) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were supplied by Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China, http://www.macklin.cn/). Ultrapure water (Milli-Q, 18.25 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experiments. The chemicals were used without additional purification. The magnetic bars and glassware were thoroughly cleaned before use.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed by using X-ray diffractometer (D-8 of Bruker) with Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ ranges from 5° to 80° by the scanning speed of 5°/min. All the florescence analyses were conducted by using a FL-4600 fluorometer (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Japan). The absorbance characterization was carried out with a UV-A390 spectrophotometer (AOE Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai). The pH values were measured with a FE-28 pH meter (Mettle Toledo international trade Co., Ltd., Shanghai). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected by Escalab250Xi. The morphology and size distribution of the samples were investigated by using Zeiss supra 55 scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL 2100 F).

Copper-doped manganese oxide (termed Cu–MnxOy) was synthesized according to previous report with minor modifications [18]. Specifically, 0.2627 g copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O) and 1 g KMnO4 were dispersed in 40 mL ultrapure water, and the mixture was kept stirring for 30 min. Next, 0.5 g tannic acid was dispersed in ultrapure water (30 mL), and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 8 by using 1 M sodium hydroxide solution. The tannic acid solution (pH 8.0, 40 mL) was slowly added to the mixed solution of potassium permanganate and copper chloride, and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. The formed mix solution was slowly poured into a Duran bottle. The mixture solution was heated at 80℃ for 12 h, followed by centrifugation and rinse with ultrapure water for five times. The Cu-MnCO3 template was obtained. The Cu-MnCO3 template was dried at 60℃ overnight, forming a brown product. Finally, 0.2 g of the product was further heated for 2 h at 500℃ to generate the black Cu-MnxOy material.

The C-dots were prepared based on a previous report with slight modifications [19]. Specifically, 0.5 mL polyethylene imide (Mw = 600) was dispersed in 9.5 mL ultrapure water. Next, 10 mL of the formed mixture solution was loaded into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 160℃ for 10 h. When the reaction finished, the oven was cooled to room temperature, and the formed suspension was placed in a refrigerator for further use.
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of C-dots was measured by the approach reported in the literature [20]. The experimental details are described in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity and kinetics study of the oxidase of Cu-MnxOy
ce:section.ce:para: The TMB (substrate of the colorimetric determination) is oxidized by oxygen dissolved in the system (acetic acid buffer), producing the blue oxTMB. The oxidase activity of Cu-MnxOy can be evaluated by the absorbance value at 652 nm. Based on these, 0.2 mL TMB solution (5 mM) and 0.1 mL Cu-MnxOy suspension (200 mg/L) were dissolved in 2 mL acetic acid buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5), and 0.1 mL C-dots suspension and 0.2 mL TMB solution (5 mM) were dissolved in 2 mL acetic acid buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5). The absorbance of the final solution was measured at 652 nm.

In order to evaluate the kinetics of Cu-MnxOy, 0.2 mL TMB with different concentrations were added to a mixture containing 0.1 mL Cu-MnxOy (200 mg/L) and 2 mL acetic acid buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5). The absorbance of these mixtures at 652 nm was recorded. The kinetic parameters (such as Vmax and Km) were obtained with the Line weaver-Burk equation: ν = Vmax × [S]/(Km + [S]), in which Vmax means the maximal reaction velocity while Km represents Michaelis constant, which is reflects the affinity of the enzyme towards the substrate.

Colorimetric detection of dopamine was performed according to the traditional colorimetric method with slight modifications [10]. The experimental system included Cu-MnxOy (0.1 mL, 2 mg/mL), TMB (0.2 mL, 5 mM), C-dots (0.1 mL) and buffer solution (acetic acid buffer 0.2 M, 2 mL), followed by the addition of different concentrations of dopamine hydrochloride solution (0.1 mL). The mixture system reacted at room temperature for 7 min. The absorbance was recorded by UV-A360. Absorbance value at 652 nm was selected, and the differences between the values of the sample and the blank control (ΔA = A0 − A) were recorded. The curve of the net absorbance at 652 nm was set as the standard curve for dopamine.

Fluorescence determination of dopamine was performed according to the traditional fluorescence detection system with minor modifications [21]. The reaction mixture included Cu-MnxOy (0.1 mL, 2 mg/mL), TMB (0.2 mL, 5 mM), C-dots (0.1 mL) and the buffer solution (acetic acid buffer 0.2 M, 2 mL), followed by the addition of different concentrations of dopamine hydrochloride solution (0.1 mL). The reaction was performed at room temperature for 7 min.

Selectivity for dopamine was tested according to the previous report [10,22]. The total solution volume was set as constant, and the dopamine was replaced with potential interferents. The interferents tested include the following: 100 μM of uric acid, urea, tryptophan, glutamate, glycine, arginine, histidine, lactic acid, fructose, sucrose, glucose, galactose and phenylalanine, and 10 μM of ascorbic acid (the content of ascorbic acid in human serum is little). Fluorescent and colorimetric analyses were performed to determine the selectivity of dopamine, and all samples were tested in triplets.
In order to evaluate the applicability of colorimetric and fluorescence detection methods, the dopamine detection in fetal bovine serum was performed by spiking blank samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Cu-MnxOy and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 exhibits the synthetic approach for Cu-MnxOy and C-dots. The crystal structure of the synthesized material was characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 , the main characteristic peaks of Cu1.5Mn1.5O4 (JCPDS 35-1172), Mn2O3 (JCPDS 41-1442) and MnO2 (JCPDS 44-0141) were found in the XRD pattern of Cu-MnxOy. This result is consistent with that of XPS. The atomic ratios of Cu/Mn and Mn/O were calculated to be 0.3 and 0.4, respectively. The morphology of Cu-MnxOy was further characterized by SEM and TEM, which displayed nanoparticle with an average size of ˜2.5 μm (Fig. 2 B, D).

As shown in the SEM image (Fig. 2C), the Cu-MnxOy material is nanoparticles, and is composed of a number of condensed rods, which could be related to hydrothermal reaction at the relatively low temperature. According to previous literature [23,24], the formation process of the Cu-MnxOy material is likely as the follows. First, CuCl2, KMnO4 and tannic acid reacted and the precursor with a rod-like morphology was formed. Then, the small rods stick together to form a dumbbell shaped material. Second, when it was calcined at 500℃ for 2 h, the shape of material was retained, likely due to the relatively lower temperature and time of calcination [18]. As shown in Fig. 2E, the doped Cu is uniformly distributed, and other elements (Mn and O) are also evenly dispersed.

The mapping image of Cu-MnxOy (Fig. 2E–H) is consistent with the results from SEM and TEM. The elemental mapping data show that the distribution of elements is uniform. The Fig. 3 A is the HRTEM image of the C-dots, which is in small circle shape and well dispersal with an average size of ˜3.8 nm (Fig. 3B). The QY of C-dots is calculated to be 11.7% with quinine sulfate as the standard (Fig. S7). The Cu-MnxOy material was analyzed by XPS to evaluate the surface composition and valence of metal cations and oxygen anions.

Fig. 4 A shows the distribution of all the elements of Cu-MnxOy as well as the binding energy. Fig. 4B exhibits the XPS spectra of Cu2p with six peaks, in which the main peaks of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 are located at 933.69 eV and 953.65 eV, respectively. Besides, there were two distinct satellite peaks at 940 eV – 950 eV and 960 eV – 970 eV, indicating the existence of CuO in Cu-MnxOy [25–27]. Furthermore, there is a 20 eV gap between Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, which further confirmed the existence of CuO [28]. Moreover, the peak within the range of 940 eV – 950 eV belongs to vibrating satellite line and the 933.69 eV peak indicates the existence of the Cu2+. A peak of 930.84 eV indicates the presence of the Cu species. Fig. 4C shows the XPS spectra of Mn2p in Cu-MnxOy with two main peaks of Mn2p1/2 at the 653.5 eV and Mn2p3/2 at 641.8 eV. The position of Mn2p3/2 was assigned to Mn(III) (638.84–645.83 eV) and Mn(Ⅳ) (639.93–648.72 eV), which shows that both Mn3+ and Mn4+ are present on the surface of Cu-MnxOy. The proportion Mn3+/Mn4+ on the surface is 0.676/0.324 [29]. Fig. 4D shows the XPS spectra of O1s. The two peaks at 529.5 eV and 532.0 eV represent two forms of O1s, namely lattice oxygen and surface hydroxyl function groups, respectively [30–32]. The peak at 529.5 eV is ascribed to Cu-O. The peak at 533.18 eV is likely due to the existence of C–O. And the ratio of Cu/Mn is 0.30.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on oxidase activity, mechanism and kinetics of Cu-MnxOy
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the oxidation activity, mechanism and enzyme kinetics of Cu-MnxOy, a set of experiments were implemented with TMB as the colorimetric substrate. For the sake of finding out whether the catalytic activity of the system depends on the Cu-MnxOy, a set of experiments were performed in the presence of TMB at the room temperature for 15 min. Fig. 5 A shows the absorbance in the presence of carbon dots or the Cu-MnxOy materials at room temperature after incubation for 15 min. The absorbance of the TMB solution increased significantly in the presence of Cu-MnxOy, while no absorbance was detected for the TMB solution with C-dots. These results indicate that only Cu-MnxOy possesses the oxidation catalytic activity in this system. In order to further elucidate the reaction mechanism, free radical scavenger is used for investigating the reactive oxygen species that may be produced during the reaction. According to the relevant literature [33,34], tert-butyl alcohol can be used to capture hydroxyl radicals, superoxide dismutase and hydroquinone can be used to remove superoxide ions, while sodium azide can be used for capturing 1O2. As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorbance intensity decreased in the presence of SOD, indicating that superoxide ions were removed and the superoxide ions is likely from the catalysis process of Cu-MnxOy. In the presence of tert-butyl alcohol, the absorbance intensity slightly decreased, demonstrating the existence of hydroxyl radicals. In view of these results, it is verified that the Cu-MnxOy material possesses oxidase-like activity. In order to obtain the optimal catalytic effect, various experimental conditions were optimized. The pH values were firstly optimized (Fig. S2), it is found that the absorbance value with pH = 3.5 was highest, thus it was chosen as the optimal pH value in the system. In addition, the reaction time were optimized as 7 min in the presence of 20 μM dopamine to obtain good stability (Fig. S3).

After two months of storage, over 90% of the catalytic activity of the Cu-Mn-O nanozyme is retained, indicating high long-term storage stability. Additionally, the reproducibility of the proposed method was investigated by preparing three batches of reaction system with the same procedures. The variances among the three batches are 1.45% and 3.81% for UV and fluorescence, respectively, which indicate good reproducibility. In order to further investigate the activity of Cu–MnxOy, the steady-state kinetic analysis was performed as a single variant. The reaction conforms to the typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics within a specific substrate concentration range. As shown Figs. S4, S5, a set of initial reaction rates were obtained and fitted with the Michaelis-Menten equation, 1/v = Km/Vmax (1/[S] + 1/Km), in which V is the initial reaction rate, Vmax means the maximum reaction rate, Km means the Michaelis constant, S represents the matrix concentration. Km values corresponding to the affinity of enzymes and substrates are calculated using the Line-weaver-Burk double reciprocal graph. The Km value was calculated to be 0.564 mM. These results show that Cu-MnxOy has good affinity and catalytic effect.

On account of the oxidase activity of Cu-MnxOy, the colorimetric biosensor by using Cu-MnxOy/C-dots was designed and investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, as a 60 μM of DA was added and the reaction was carried out for 7 min, the absorbance of the TMB solution at 652 nm reduced. This means that the presence of DA can inhibit the oxidase-like activity of Cu-MnxOy. Based on this result, a convenient colorimetric sensing system was fabricated to determine the DA level. Fig. 7 A shows that the visual color changes as well as dose-response curve of DA under the optimal condition. With the concentration of DA increased from 0 to 100 μM, the absorbance of the system gradually decreases. The inhibition of absorbance (ΔA at 652 nm) is linearly correlated with the DA concentration in the range from 0 to 5 μM (R2 = 0.969), 5–75 μM (R2 = 0.990) (Fig. 7C). The detection limit (LOD, 3σ) was found to be 0.5 μM, comparable to or better than the LOD by other methods (Table 1 ). The wide linear range for determination of DA is beneficial for clinical and medical applications.

In order to test the feasibility of the combination of Cu-MnxOy and carbon dots system, a set of experiments were implemented. As shown in Fig. 6B, C-dots in 360 nm excitation wavelength displayed strong fluorescence with the emission wavelength at 464 nm. When TMB was mixed with a C-dots (bluish violet line in Fig. 6B), fluorescence emission at 464 nm exhibited no significant change. Compared to that with solely C-dots, another peak appeared, which is likely due to TMB itself. As the TMB/Cu–MnxOy (black line in the Fig. 6B) was mixed with the C-dots solution (0.2 M, pH = 3.5 acetate buffer), the fluorescence was quenched compared with that of solely C-dots solution. This is attributed to the oxidation of TMB (oxTMB) [21], of which the absorption have overlap with the emission peaks of 464 nm from the C-dots, and the fluorescence was quenched by oxTMB due to the fluorescence inner filtering effect to the C-dots (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. S8A, oxTMB has an absorption peak at 460 nm. In Fig. S8B, the red line (a) shows the absorption of oxTMB, while the black line (b) shows the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots. The absorption peaks of oxTMB is at 460 nm, which overlaps with the emission peak of C-dots. Therefore, based on fluorescence internal filtering effect, the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots can be quenched [21,35]. Fig. 6B depicts the fluorescence quenching of the C-dots as the TMB oxidation was suppressed by DA. With increasing DA concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually increases (Fig. 8 A). As shown in Fig. 8B, the fluorescence response is linearly correlated with the DA concentration in the range of 0–100 μM (R2 = 0.9918) with the low LOD of 0.125 μM (S/N = 3). Moreover, it is verified that compared with other detection method, the present fluorescence method possesses much wider liner range and a lower detection limit (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the system to dopamine
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the system for dopamine is tested. As shown in Fig. S6, common interferents were tested, including uric acid, urea, tryptophan, glutamate, glycine, arginine, histidine, lactic acid, fructose, sucrose, glucose, galactose, phenylalanine, acarbose, glutamic acid, glycerol, phenol, dichlorophenol and resorcinol at the concentration of 100 μM, bovine serum albumin at 50 g/L, ascorbic acid, 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic and dopa at 10 μM. As the concentration of ascorbic acid and dopa in serum is very low, lower concentration of ascorbic acid and dopa were used for testing its interference to dopamine. The results show that these compounds do not have appreciable interference to the determination of DA, thus this method has high selectivity toward DA determination [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of dopamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of this colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal method, DA detection in fetal bovine serum was performed by spiking blank samples. The fetal bovine serum sample was diluted for 10 times with 0.2 mL acetic acid buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5) and spiked with DA at known concentration. 5 mM of TMB, 200 mg/L of Cu-MnxOy (0.2 ml) and C-dots (0.1 ml) were added to the mixture. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 7 min. The absorbance value at 652 nm were recorded for the calculation of the concentration of the DA. The results show that the recovery ranges in 94%–106.8% with the RSD values less than 7.7%, which indicate that this method can be used in clinical sample detection with good accuracy for DA (Table S1).

In conclusion, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal determination method with nano-enzyme materials and C-dots was developed for determination of DA. The Cu-Mn-O microcrystals were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction and calcination, which exhibit excellent oxidase activity. The colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal system was constructed based on the fluorescence internal filtering effect. The preparation process is convenient, of low-cost and nontoxic. For the colorimetric method, the linear range is 0–75 μM with the LOD of 0.5 μM. For the fluorescence method, the linear range is 0–100 μM with the LOD of 0.125 μM. This convenient and sensitive method shows great potential in application for determination of DA in biomedical samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 03570113), the Education Department of Sichuan Province, P. R. China (Grant No. 16ZA0039). The financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11404358) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.03.107.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An integrative perspective on the role of dopamine in schizophrenia
Biol. Psychiat.
Interactions of iron, dopamine and neuromelanin pathways in brain aging and Parkinson’s disease
Prog. Neurobiol.
Oxidase-mimicking activity of perovskite LaMnO3+δ nanofibers and their application for colorimetric sensing
J. Mater. Chem. B
Simultaneous voltammetric determination of dopamine and uric acid using carbon-encapsulated hollow Fe3O4 nanoparticles anchored to an electrode modified with nanosheets of reduced graphene oxide
Microchim. Acta
A versatile DNA detection scheme based on the quenching of fluorescent silver nanoclusters by MoS2 nanosheets: application to aptamer-based determination of hepatitis B virus and of dopamine
Microchim. Acta
Silicon quantum dot involved luminol chemiluminescence and its sensitive detection of dopamine
Anal. Methods

Determination of dopamine concentrations in brain extracellular fluid using microdialysis with short sampling intervals, analyzed by ultra high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry
J. Pharmacol. Toxicol.
Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles
Nat. Nano Technol.
A 3-dimensional C/CeO2 hollow nanostructure framework as a peroxidase mimetic, and its application to the colorimetric determination of hydrogen peroxide
Microchim. Acta
